# Hypothesis tests


## Errors

Assume we want to detect a signal in order to check whether a certain thing happens. There are 4 possibilities:

- It happens and we detect the signal: True positive
- It happens and we fail to detect the signal: False negative
- It doesn't happen and we didn't detect the signal: True negative
- It doesn't happen and we detect a signal: False positive

|                       | Detect signal        | Do not detect signal     |
|-----------------------|----------------------|--------------------------|
| **It happens**        | True positive (TP)   | False negative (FN)      |
| **It doesn't happen** | False positive (FP)  | True negative (TN)       |

Both false positives and false negatives are errors. 

- False Positive is called **Type I error**
- False Negative is called **Type II error**

When doing hypothesis test, usually the null hypothesis is the default case, and we need to detect a signal to accept the alternative hypothesis. Therefore we might make mistakes. Since $H_0$ represents the default case, we could rewrite the above table as follows:

|                       | Reject $H_0$        | Fail to reject $H_0$     |
|-----------------------|----------------------|--------------------------|
| **$H_0$ is false**        | True positive (TP)   | False negative (FN)      |
| **$H_0$ is true** | False positive (FP)  | True negative (TN)       |



::: {#def-}
## p-value 
The probability, assuming $H_0$ is true, of observing a test statistic at least as extreme as the one observed is called **p-value**.
$$
\text{p-value}=\Pr(\text{data or more extreme}\mid H_0)=\Pr(s\geq s_{\alpha}\mid H_0),
$$
where $s$ is a test statistic, and $s_{\alpha}$ is the observed test statistic.
:::



::: {#def-}
## Significance level ($\alpha$)
The **significance level** is the pre-chosen probability of committing a Type I error. 
$$
\alpha=\Pr(\text{reject } H_0\mid H_0\text{ is true}).
$$
:::



A test use a test statistic $T$, and large values of $T$ are evidence against $H_0$. Assume we choose a cut off $c_{\alpha}$ so that once we have $T>c$, we will reject $H_0$. Then the probability of Type I errors are
$$
\Pr(T>c_{\alpha}\mid H_0)=\Pr(\text{reject } H_0\mid H_0\text{ is true})=\alpha.
$$

::: {.callout-note}
A hypothesis test with rejection region $R$ has significance level $\alpha$ if
$$
\Pr(T\in R\mid H_0)\leq \alpha.
$$
If the rejection region is chosen so that
$$
\Pr(T\in R\mid H_0)= \alpha,
$$
then the test achieves Type I error probability exactly $\alpha$. Equivalently, for a continuous test, if we reject whenever $p\leq \alpha$, then
$$
\Pr(p\leq \alpha\mid H_0)= \alpha,
$$
:::


::: {#thm-pvalue-level}
## Theorem

For a continuous hypothesis test, if we reject $H_0$ whenever $p \le \alpha$, then

$$
P(p \le \alpha \mid H_0)=\alpha.
$$

Hence the test has Type I error probability exactly $\alpha$.
:::

::: {#prf-}
## Proof

Suppose $T$ is a test statistic for which large values provide evidence against $H_0$, and let $F_0$ be the cumulative distribution function of $T$ under $H_0$. Since the test is continuous, $F_0$ is continuous.

For an observed value $t_{\mathrm{obs}}$, the p-value is

$$
p=P(T\ge t_{\mathrm{obs}}\mid H_0)=1-F_0(t_{\mathrm{obs}}).
$$

Therefore, under $H_0$, the random p-value can be written as

$$
p=1-F_0(T).
$$

Then

$$
P(p\le \alpha\mid H_0)
=
P(1-F_0(T)\le \alpha\mid H_0)
=
P(F_0(T)\ge 1-\alpha\mid H_0).
$$

By the probability integral transform, since $F_0$ is continuous,

$$
F_0(T)\sim \text{Uniform}(0,1)
\qquad\text{under }H_0.
$$

Thus

$$
P(F_0(T)\ge 1-\alpha)=\alpha.
$$

Hence

$$
P(p\le \alpha\mid H_0)=\alpha.
$$

Therefore the test achieves Type I error probability exactly $\alpha$.
:::
